message.content:
You are reviewing Angular components for PERFORMANCE and PAGE-LOAD issues ONLY (not correctness). Read the ACTUAL code, do not guess.

Review these in C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend:
- src/app/pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.ts (and .html)
- src/app/pages/itemDetail/license-tracking/license-tracking.component.ts
- src/app/pages/art-piece/art-piece/art-piece.component.ts (and .html)
- src/app/pages/art-piece/art-piece-detail/art-piece-detail.component.ts (and .html)
- src/app/pages/manage_users/manage_users.component.ts (and .html)
- src/app/pages/dashboards/dashboard-analytics/ (all)

Look specifically for:
1. RxJS subscriptions never unsubscribed (memory leaks) — subscribe() without cleanup.
2. Nested subscribes, sequential HTTP awaits that could be parallel (forkJoin/Promise.all), N+1 request patterns (HTTP in loops/forEach), duplicate HTTP calls on init.
3. Functions/getters bound in HTML templates that run every change-detection cycle (expensive ones returning filtered/mapped arrays).
4. Missing trackBy on *ngFor over large lists.
5. Missing changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush.
6. ag-grid: heavy cellRenderers, rowData rebuilt expensively, no virtualization.
7. Heavy synchronous work in ngOnInit/constructor blocking render.

For each finding report: severity (High/Med/Low), absolute file path WITH line number, 1-2 sentence problem description citing the real code, and a concrete fix. Rank by impact. Return ONLY the findings list, no preamble. Be concrete — cite actual variable/method names and line numbers.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I have everything I need. Here are the findings, ranked by impact.

## Performance & Page-Load Findings

### HIGH

**1. itemDetail: all init HTTP requests run sequentially instead of in parallel (slow page load)**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\pages\itemDetail\itemDetail.component.ts` lines 210–472 (`ngOnInit`).
The init awaits a long chain one-after-another: `getDivisionCode()` (215) → `getSeasonCode` (221) → `loadCustomerOptions()` (228) → `getMerchGroup` subscribe (230), and inside that callback further sequential awaits `getCompanyCode` (420), `getAllVendors` (445), `getItemDepth()` (452), `getItemInfo()` (454). Most of these are independent. Each request blocks the next, multiplying total time-to-interactive. Fix: run independent calls with `forkJoin`/`Promise.all` (e.g. season, customers, company code, vendors, depth in parallel), only chaining what genuinely depends on `divisionCode` or `itemData`.

**2. itemDetail: `getMerchGroup` mixes `await` with `.subscribe`, and dependent calls live inside the subscribe callback**
Same file, line 230: `await this.itemService.getMerchGroup(...).subscribe(async (data) => {...})`. `await` on a Subscription is meaningless, and the heavy nested-loop processing (235–401) plus all downstream awaits run inside the callback, deepening the sequential chain and nesting `getCompanyCode`, `getUDFElements` subscribe (429), `getAllVendors`, `getItemInfo` etc. Fix: convert to `firstValueFrom`, hoist independent calls out, flatten the chain.

**3. itemDetail: `getItemInfo` (the item) is fetched twice on load**
Same file: `getDivisionCode()` (line 195) calls `this.ItemDetailService.getItemInfo({ id })` and stores `this.itemData`; then `getItemInfo()` (line 454) consumes that same `itemData`. But `openViewUploadedFilesDialog` (707) and the image-upload `(refreshItemData)="getItemInfo()"` re-fetch. More importantly, within init `getItemInfo()` then fires two more requests — `getMG` (566) and `GetLicensingStatus` (590) — serially after everything else. These should be parallelized with the main batch. Confirm the initial item fetch isn't duplicated by child components hitting the same endpoint.

**4. itemDetail HTML: expensive getters `timelineItems` and `teamMembers` bound in template, recomputed every change-detection cycle**
`itemDetail.component.html` line 100 (`@for (m of timelineItems...)`) and line 956 (`@for (member of teamMembers...)`). Both are getters in the .ts (lines 924 and 937) that build/map new arrays on every call. Because they return new array references, Angular's default CD re-renders these loops every cycle, and the getter bodies (`Date.now()`, `.map`) run constantly. Fix: compute once into a plain field when `itemData`/assignments change, or make them pure memoized values; ideally also adopt OnPush.

**5. license-tracking: N+1 / per-row work plus heavy synchronous processing in `updateRowData`**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\pages\itemDetail\license-tracking\license-tracking.component.ts` lines 149–208. For each row it calls `enrichRowWithGroupInfo` (325) which does `this.groupsList.find(...)` and, per group member, `this.usersList.find(...)` (332–335) — nested linear scans = O(rows × members × users). On large comment lists this is quadratic/cubic. Fix: build `Map` lookups for `groupsList` by id and `usersList` by id once, then O(1) lookups.

### MEDIUM

**6. No `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` on any reviewed component**
`itemDetail.component.ts` (32), `license-tracking.component.ts` (16), `art-piece.component.ts` (44), `art-piece-detail.component.ts` (23), `manage_users.component.ts` (29), `dashboard-analytics.component.ts` (11). All use default CD. Combined with template getters (#4) and heavy templates (itemDetail has hundreds of bindings across three attribute sets), this makes every async event re-evaluate the entire view. Fix: adopt OnPush where feasible, especially itemDetail and art-piece-detail.

**7. license-tracking: subscriptions never unsubscribed (memory leak)**
Same file. `getAllUsers` (101), `GetLicensingStatus` (109), `loadLicensingTimeline` (139), plus dialog/`updateRowData` follow-ups — all `.subscribe(...)` with no `takeUntil`/teardown and no `ngOnDestroy`. Component is created/destroyed each time the Licensing tab is opened, leaking. Fix: add `destroy$` + `takeUntil`, implement `ngOnDestroy`.

**8. itemDetail: subscriptions in init/`getItemInfo` not torn down**
`itemDetail.component.ts`: `getMerchGroup` (230), `getUDFElements` (429), `getMG` (566), `GetLicensingStatus` (590), `route.queryParams` (466) — none unsubscribed; no `ngOnDestroy`. The `route.queryParams` subscription in particular is a classic leak. Fix: `takeUntil(destroy$)` + `ngOnDestroy`.

**9. art-piece-detail: sequential awaits in `ngOnInit` + cascading re-fetch of full data after every mutation**
`C:\repos\dflow\designflow-frontend\src\app\pages\art-piece\art-piece-detail\art-piece-detail.component.ts` lines 112–127: `getSeasonCode` → `getCompanyCode` → `getArtPieceInfo()` → `getRelatedItems()` all awaited serially; season/company/art-piece are independent and should be `forkJoin`/`Promise.all`. Also every `setPrimaryImage` (301), `deleteImage` (330), `onUploadComplete` (382), `onSave` (415) calls `getArtPieceInfo()` which itself re-fetches art piece AND re-fetches `getMerchGroup` (line 145) — reloading the whole merch-group dataset just to refresh an image list. Fix: parallelize init; on image mutations refresh only attachments, not merch groups. Uses `.toPromise()` (deprecated per CLAUDE.md) at 117, 118, 131, 145, 265.

**10. art-piece-detail HTML: `getPrimaryImage(item)` function called in template inside `@for`**
`art-piece-detail.component.html` lines 306–307: `@if (getPrimaryImage(item))` then `[src]="getPrimaryImage(item)"` — the function (ts line 427, does `.find`) runs twice per related item per CD cycle. Fix: precompute a `primaryImageUrl` field on each related item in `getRelatedItems()`.

**11. art-piece: `convertToItem` does two `this.rowData.find(...)` scans for the same row**
`art-piece.component.ts` lines 748–749: `this.rowData.find(data => data.id === artPiece.id)` called twice back-to-back. Minor, but trivially dedupable into one lookup.

### LOW

**12. manage_users: HTTP call fired from constructor**
`manage_users.component.ts` line 177: `getCustomGridLayoutList(...).subscribe(...)` runs in the constructor (also not unsubscribed). Constructor-time I/O blocks component construction and runs before lifecycle. Fix: move to `ngOnInit`, add teardown.

**13. manage_users: `onBtExport` does sequential `await getBase64FromUrl` per row in a loop (N+1 fetches)**
`manage_users.component.ts` lines 612–617: loops over all `rowData` awaiting an image fetch one at a time. (Also each iteration overwrites `window['base64Images']` with `Object.assign({...})` rather than merging, so it's both slow and buggy.) Same pattern in art-piece `onBtExport` (lines 1011–1023). Fix: `Promise.all` the fetches; accumulate into one object. Export-time only, hence Low for page-load.

**14. manage_users: dialog `afterClosed().subscribe` calls never unsubscribed**
`manage_users.component.ts` lines 245, 250, 255, 261 and others. `mat-dialog` afterClosed completes after emit so leak risk is low, but there is no `ngOnDestroy` and `destroy$` (declared line 104) is never `.next()/.complete()`d, so the `gridUISetupService.bootstrap(this.gridApi, this.destroy$)` teardown (line 737) never fires — a real leak of grid UI subscriptions.

**15. manage_users: no `trackBy` and full `rowData` rebuild on every filter change**
`onStatusChange` (884), `onSignUpStatusChange` (909), `onUserTypeChange` (899) each refetch all users and `setGridOption('rowData', data)` with no row id reuse beyond `getRowNodeId`. Acceptable for ag-grid (it has its own virtualization), but `onUserTypeChange` toggles `isGridVisible` off/on via `setTimeout(...,0)` (903–905), destroying and recreating the entire grid on every user-type switch — costly. Fix: just reload data instead of recreating the grid.

**16. art-piece / art-piece-detail / itemDetail `@for` tracking by object identity**
e.g. art-piece-detail.component.html `track option`, `track data`, `track item` (lines 7, 20, 152, 301...), and itemDetail edit selects `track value` (455, 466, etc.). Tracking by the object reference is effectively no trackBy benefit if arrays are re-sorted/re-created (they are, via `customSort` returning new arrays). For the option lists this defeats reuse. Low impact given list sizes. Fix: `track option.id`.

Notes: `manage_users` and `art-piece` correctly use `forkJoin` for their grid init (good). `dashboard-analytics.component.ts` is essentially static demo data with an empty template and a no-op `setTimeout` in `ngOnInit` (lines 92–104) — no real perf issue, but the component renders nothing useful and the chart series are hardcoded. ag-grid usage in `art-piece` is server-side row model with proper virtualization; `manage_users` uses client-side with masterDetail and a DOM-built `cellRenderer` for the impersonate button (lines 302–333) created per row — acceptable but a heavier renderer than a string template.
